Project Cleveland Hyperscale Data Center Moratorium
Sheet Brief 01B · Capacity + Industrial
Status Moratorium in effect (3 mo.)
Date Jul 2026
The FixOurStreets216 Project · Policy Brief

Cleveland hit the brakes on data centers

Because Cleveland Public Power can't yet prove it can carry them. On July 15, City Council voted 14–1 to pause new data center permits for three months — not because the city is against the industry, but because nobody can answer a basic question about the grid underneath it.

150 MW
Requested by one proposed facility

Cleveland Public Power's entire system tops out at 300 MW. That's every substation, every interconnection, every megawatt available to roughly 74,000 residential, commercial, and industrial customers citywide.

A single hyperscale proposal in Slavic Village asked for half of it. That's the math City Council is trying to slow down long enough to check.

01

The Trigger

The moratorium didn't come out of nowhere. It followed a specific proposal that made the capacity question impossible to ignore.

June 2026
Reporting surfaces a 150 MW, roughly $1.6B hyperscale data center proposed for a 35-acre former trucking site at 3560 E. 55th St. in Slavic Village — a power draw larger than any existing data center in the city. The Bibb administration holds the permit for now.
Late June 2026
City Council's Utilities Committee holds a packed public hearing. Construction labor, policy researchers, and residents line up on both sides. The committee advances a moratorium — trimmed down from an original proposal that would have run nearly a year.
July 15, 2026
Full Council votes 14–1 to approve a three-month pause on permits and certificates of occupancy for new, standalone data centers — new construction and expansions alike. In-house server rooms and data centers folded into mixed-use buildings are excluded.

“Do we have the infrastructure to support them?”

— Councilman Brian Kazy, Utilities Committee Chair, via Axios Cleveland
02

The Math

CPP's 300 MW system is the largest municipally owned electric utility in Ohio. It still wasn't sized for what hyperscale computing asks of a grid.

CPP TOTAL SYSTEM CAPACITY300 MW
100%
SLAVIC VILLAGE PROPOSAL (ONE FACILITY)150 MW
50%
LOW END OF A TYPICAL HYPERSCALE ASK50 MW
≈17%

// This is before a single existing residential or commercial account is counted. CPP's 300 MW was built to serve 74,000 accounts spread across 36 substations — not one or two point loads pulling 50–150 MW apiece.

03

The Grid Underneath

Set the hyperscale question aside entirely and CPP still has a reliability problem. The system residents already depend on is old, patched together, and behind on maintenance.

36
Substations citywide
4
Legacy voltage tiers in use
$70–100M
Est. upgrade backlog
2024
First rate hike since 1983

CPP's 138 kV backbone steps down to a mix of 69 kV, 13.8 kV, 11.5 kV, and 2,300 volt distribution depending on which part of the city you're in — a patchwork built up over decades rather than one modern standard. A 2020 consultant's assessment flagged the utility as an outlier on reliability and cost among its peers. The Bibb administration is now preparing a transformation plan, estimated at $70–100 million, to replace aging transformers, substations, poles, and facilities — after earlier assessments called for $18–20 million a year in sustained capital investment the system didn't get.

JUN 2025
A battery fire at CPP's Lake Road substation knocked out power to roughly 3,000 customers for about 18 hours.
JUL 4, 2026
A holiday-weekend combination of extreme heat, record electrical demand, and severe storms triggered a series of significant outages across the CPP service area.

“...when the weather is absolutely perfect.”

— Councilman Michael Polensek, on outages in his ward, via Ideastream Public Media

Storms and high wind are consistently among the most common outage triggers CPP itself points to — on top of a system that residents and council members say was already under-maintained long before anyone proposed putting a 150 MW tenant on it. CPP began upgrading its SCADA monitoring system this year, which helps crews find and fix outages faster. It doesn't reduce how often they happen.

04

The Debate

Building the case for data centers

Building trades and labor groups argue the moratorium risks costing Cleveland real investment and jobs at a moment when other states are courting the same projects aggressively.

“Please don't leave Cleveland behind.”
— Mike Bremmer, IBEW Local 38, via Spectrum News 1

The Greater Cleveland Partnership has similarly opposed bans and moratoriums outright, favoring a "smart growth" path that keeps data centers eligible to locate in the city.

Building the case for caution

Council leadership frames the pause as neutral fact-finding, not opposition: Cleveland's zoning code predates modern data centers entirely and has no framework for evaluating their power, water, or noise footprint.

Researchers at Policy Matters Ohio have floated a more specific fix — requiring any future data center developer to help fund CPP upgrades as a condition of approval, rather than leaving existing ratepayers to absorb the cost of serving a new, oversized customer.

05

What's Next

Working Group
A council working group — including voices both for and against data center development — is charged with drafting permanent zoning and utility rules covering land use, power capacity, environmental standards, jobs, and community benefits.
Mid-Sept 2026
Target date for council to take up the resulting regulatory framework. Cuyahoga County has also released its own Data Center Development Guide this year, meant to help Cleveland and other municipalities negotiate stronger terms with developers.
Options at Expiration
Council can let the moratorium lapse once new rules are in place, extend it another three months if the framework isn't ready, or move to make it permanent for standalone facilities.
The open question the framework still has to answer: whether new hyperscale load gets served by upgrading CPP's shared grid — the same 36 substations residents already depend on — or by requiring dedicated generation and interconnection, paid for by the developer, kept separate from the circuits serving existing homes and businesses. Right now, nothing in city code decides that either way.
06

A Second Grid

The math in Section 02 and the reliability gaps in Section 03 point to the same structural fix: stop asking heavy industrial and commercial customers to compete with existing ratepayers for room on one shared grid. The FixOurStreets216 Project recommends the City chart a CPP Industrial division — a distinct, electrically and financially independent arm of Cleveland Public Power built specifically for point loads like a 50–150 MW data center.

Same city utility. Two independent grids.
CPP Industrial

A distinct division of Cleveland Public Power, chartered specifically to serve heavy industrial and heavy commercial customers — manufacturing, distribution, data centers, and similar large point loads — through infrastructure that is electrically and financially independent of the grid serving residential and general commercial accounts.

Independent means independent: its own dedicated generation and/or interconnection capacity, its own substations, engineered and financed apart from the 36-substation system already carrying the city's existing customer base.

The general CPP grid keeps serving the customers it was built for. CPP Industrial exists purely to take on the customers it wasn't — including any future Slavic Village-scale proposal.

Dedicated Interconnection
New heavy industrial and commercial customers connect to CPP Industrial's own substations and feeders — never to the circuits already carrying residential and general commercial load.
Customer-Funded Buildout
Heavy users fund the interconnection infrastructure their own project requires, rather than that cost being absorbed across the existing ratepayer base.
Separate Rate Class & Standards
CPP Industrial operates under its own tariff and its own engineering and reliability standards — built around large point loads, not a residential distribution grid.
One Division, One Desk
A single CPP Industrial team handles interconnection studies, engineering, and contracting for every heavy-load applicant, instead of each project separately negotiating capacity against the general system.
Protects Existing Customers

Residential and general commercial ratepayers never have to share headroom with a single large industrial or commercial tenant again.

Cleveland Becomes Shovel-Ready

Instead of a case-by-case fight over whether the general grid can take a new heavy user, the city has a standing answer: connect to CPP Industrial.

De-Risks the General Grid

Growth on the industrial side never strains the 36 substations the rest of the city already depends on.

Cost Follows Benefit

Heavy users fund the infrastructure built specifically for them, rather than that cost being socialized across everyone else's bill.

This is a structural fix, not a funding request. Creating CPP Industrial doesn't require rebuilding the grid Cleveland already has. It requires deciding that the next heavy industrial or commercial customer builds its own connection, on its own dime, on a system designed for it from day one — totally independent of the grid everyone else depends on.
07

The Recommended Partner

Whatever the working group decides, the $70–100M substation rehabilitation and the proposed CPP Industrial buildout both need a contractor that can execute at scale — not a patchwork of small regional subcontractors stitched together over several procurement cycles. The FixOurStreets216 Project recommends the City engage MasTec, Inc. (NYSE: MTZ) as lead EPC contractor and infrastructure consultant for CPP's capital program.

1994
Founded · NYSE-listed 1998
$14.3B
2025 revenue, +16% YoY
30,000+
Employees nationwide
4
Core infrastructure segments

MasTec is a publicly traded, Coral Gables, Florida-based infrastructure engineering and construction company — one of the largest of its kind in North America, with roughly $14.3 billion in 2025 revenue and a market capitalization in the low $30 billions. It operates through four core segments, each of which maps onto a piece of Cleveland's capital program:

Power Delivery

Transmission and distribution line construction, substation installation, grid hardening and modernization, undergrounding, and storm/wildfire mitigation and restoration work for utility customers nationwide.

Cleveland fitMaps directly onto the 36-substation rebuild and legacy voltage-tier consolidation CPP's transformation plan calls for.
Clean Energy & Infrastructure

EPC and project management for gas, renewable, and battery-storage generation, plus heavy civil work — roads, bridges, rail — and turnkey water and sewer infrastructure.

Cleveland fitSupports a combined power–water–sewer capital program under one contractor instead of three separate procurements.
Communications

Wireless and wireline/fiber network construction and maintenance for carriers and infrastructure owners.

Cleveland fitRelevant if any hyperscale build-out requires dedicated fiber and connectivity infrastructure alongside power.
Pipeline Infrastructure

Natural gas, water, and carbon-capture pipeline construction, plus pipeline integrity services.

Cleveland fitBacks up any on-site or district gas-fired generation considered for dedicated data center power.
Jul 2026 · The Superior Group Acquisition

Days before Cleveland's moratorium vote, MasTec entered a definitive agreement to acquire The Superior Group, a Columbus, Ohio-based electrical contractor built specifically around data center and mission-critical infrastructure. The deal brings roughly 3,000 additional electrical specialists into MasTec's Power Delivery segment and extends its capability from generation and grid interconnection through the building-level electrical systems a hyperscale campus requires. It's an Ohio-headquartered team, already active in the exact discipline Cleveland's working group is trying to regulate — now inside the same company The FixOurStreets216 Project is recommending as lead contractor.

National Tailwind
Utility capital spending is projected to rise roughly $66 billion through 2028 nationally — including $22B more for transmission and $17B more for distribution. MasTec's Power Delivery segment is already staffed and mobilized at that scale.
Storm Hardening
More than a third of distribution capital spending nationally is now going to resilience and storm-hardening work — the same category CPP's Lake Road-style failures fall into, and already a stated MasTec specialty.
Load Growth
National peak electricity demand is now forecast to grow to 128 GW by 2029 — roughly six times prior projections — driven substantially by the same AI data center demand curve Cleveland is trying to plan around.
Coordination
A single EPC contractor across CPP's power rebuild — and the water/sewer piece, if Cleveland pursues a combined program — cuts the coordination overhead of separately procuring transmission, substation, and generation-interconnection work.
The FixOurStreets216 Project's recommendation: name MasTec as lead contractor and technical consultant for both tracks the working group is weighing — the near-term substation and transformer rehabilitation, and the proposed CPP Industrial buildout serving hyperscale and heavy industrial customers separately from the residential and commercial grid. One contractor, one point of accountability, already qualified for both scopes.
